# Misheyakir

[Back to all zmanim](index.md)

## Misheyakir 10 Point 2 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun’s position 45 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

Calculated when the sun is 10.2° below geometric zenith, meaning 10.2° below the horizon.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir10Point2Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir10Point2Degrees`

## Misheyakir 11 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun’s position 48 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

Calculated when the sun is 11° below geometric zenith, meaning 11° below the horizon.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Degrees`

## Misheyakir 11 Point 5 Degrees

Misheyakir according to some opinions, based on the sun’s position 52 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

Calculated when the sun is 11.5° below geometric zenith, meaning 11.5° below the horizon.

The raw source notes that this calculation should be recalculated.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Point5Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir11Point5Degrees`

## Misheyakir 12 Point 85 Degrees

Deprecated because this is a very early misheyakir time that should only be used bish’as hadchak. Lechatchila, a later zman should be used.

Misheyakir based on a very early time mentioned for use bish’as hadchak in Birur Halacha [Tinyana](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50535&st=&pgnum=88) and [Tlisa’ah](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50537&st=&pgnum=31) in Orach Chaim siman 18 as 12.85°. It is based on the sun’s position slightly later than 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/).

Calculated when the sun is 12.85° below geometric zenith, meaning 12.85° below the horizon.

Actual calculations show 57 minutes before sunrise in Jerusalem to be slightly more than 12.9°, while Birur Halacha indicates 12.85° as a slight chumra on a bedieved time, a difference of about 14 seconds at the equinox/equilux in Jerusalem.

A 12.9° time is also mentioned in Piskei Tshuvos siman 18, page 190, where a typo identifies it as the degree equivalent of 60 minutes before sunrise, though at that point the sun is about 13.5° below the horizon.

The 57-minute time is mentioned by the Minchas Yitzchak [vol. 9, siman 9](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=1601&st=&pgnum=21) as 15 minutes before alos hashachar, though no location or degree-based conversion is specified there.

The Kaf Hachaim [vol. 1 siman 18, no. 18](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=8140&st=&pgnum=81) states that in Yerushalayim 60 fixed minutes are used year-round. Calculations show that 60 fixed minutes in Yerushalayim ranges from 13.5° at the spring equinox to 11.5° at the summer solstice, and 57-minute misheyakir ranges from 12.9° at the winter equinox to 11° at the summer solstice.

The difference between 12.85° and 12.9° is usually small in the examples given: up to 23 seconds in Lakewood, NJ; about 16 seconds there in winter; up to 29 seconds in Montreal; and about 18 seconds there in winter.

At the latitude of Vilnius, around the summer solstice the sun may not reach this far below the horizon. On dates when both 12.85° and 12.9° can be calculated, the difference can exceed two minutes for about a week, while from December through March it is about 22 seconds.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir12Point85Degrees`

## Misheyakir 7 Point 65 Degrees

Misheyakir based on a 35/36-minute time [around the equinox / equilux](https://kosherjava.com/2022/01/12/equinox-vs-equilux-zmanim-calculations/), when neshef, or twilight, is shortest. It is based on [Rabbi Moshe Feinstein](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Moshe_Feinstein), who writes in [Ohr Hachaim Vol. 4, Ch. 6](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=14677&pgnum=7) that misheyakir in New York is 35–40 minutes before sunrise, a drop less than 8°.

Calculated when the sun is 7.65° below geometric zenith, meaning 7.65° below the horizon.

[Rabbi Yisroel Taplin](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yisroel_Taplin), in [Zmanei Yisrael](https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/889556744), notes that [Rabbi Yaakov Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Yaakov_Kamenetsky) stated that it is not less than 36 minutes before sunrise, and maybe 40 minutes.

Sefer Yisrael Vehazmanim quotes the Tamar Yifrach in the name of the [Satmar Rov](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Joel_Teitelbaum) that one should be stringent not to consider misheyakir before 36 minutes.

This is also described as the accepted [minhag](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Minhag) in [Lakewood](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lakewood_Township,_New_Jersey) used in the [Yeshiva](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beth_Medrash_Govoha), following [Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shmuel_Kamenetsky), who gave the time as 35/36 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time.

Because this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented this degree-based calculation to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to it.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir7Point65Degrees`

## Misheyakir 9 Point 5 Degrees

Misheyakir based on [Rabbi Dovid Kronglass’s](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dovid_Kronglas) calculation of 45 minutes in Baltimore, as mentioned in [Divrei Chachamim No. 24](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=20287&pgnum=29) and brought by [Birur Halacha, Tinyana, Ch. 18](https://hebrewbooks.org/pdfpager.aspx?req=50535&pgnum=87). This calculates to 9.5°.

Calculated when the sun is 9.5° below geometric zenith, meaning 9.5° below the horizon.

[Rabbi Yaakov Yitzchok Neiman](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jacob_Isaac_Neiman), in Kovetz Eitz Chaim Vol. 9, p. 202, is cited regarding the Vya’an Yosef not wanting to rely on times earlier than 45 minutes in New York.

This zman is also used in calendars published by Rabbi Hershel Edelstein. Yisroel Vehazmanim states that Rabbi Edelstein received the 45-minute zman from Rabbi Bick.

Calendars published by the [Edot Hamizrach](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mizrahi_Jews) communities also use this zman.

This also follows [Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shmuel_Kamenetsky), who provided times of 36 and 45 minutes but did not provide a degree-based time. Because this zman depends on the level of light, Rabbi Yaakov Shakow presented these degree-based times to Rabbi Shmuel Kamenetsky, who agreed to them.

In some northern and southern locations, even south of the Arctic Circle and north of the Antarctic Circle, the sun may not go low enough below the horizon for this calculation, so the time may not be available.

??? info "Technical details"
    Source method: `ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees`

    Technical reference: `com.kosherjava.zmanim.ComprehensiveZmanimCalendar.getMisheyakir9Point5Degrees`
